entries.author: Amin
entries.content:
## From Payload to Sveltia CMS

My research into how to build a dynamic and free website started with Payload CMS. I had already learned it and honestly thought it was incredible. The plan was to use Neon's free tier for PostgreSQL as the database — that way, I could run a full-featured CMS completely for free. On top of that, it would let Omid build Payload block by block for future projects.

But unfortunately, based on what I found out, deploying Payload on Vercel comes with a lot of headaches. So I switched to a different approach — Sveltia CMS, which is a Git-based CMS. It's essentially still a dynamic site, but with a lightweight panel where we can upload and manage our content. For our situation right now — with all the limitations and restrictions we're dealing with — this approach is more than enough.

Along the way, I pushed to make the site as dynamic as possible, especially giving us real flexibility inside the blog. Thankfully, that part went well and it's working the way I imagined.

Then came deployment and debugging — pushing the project to GitHub and deploying it on Vercel. And honestly, that part is still held up because of the internet.
